stem:
A 5-week-old infant born at 37 weeks presents to the emergency department with 3 days of coryza, worsening work of breathing, and feeding at about a third of normal volume. The parents report fewer wet nappies in the last 12 hours and a quiet, less interactive baby.

On arrival he is alert but irritable with audible wheeze and moderate subcostal and intercostal recessions. There is no apnoea witnessed in the cubicle. Bedside viral PCR is positive for RSV. Chest x-ray shows hyperinflation only.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 168 /min, blood pressure 78/46 mmHg, respiratory rate 70 /min, SpO2 88% on room air, temperature 37.4 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: alert but irritable, subcostal and intercostal recessions, audible wheeze with widespread fine crackles, capillary refill 2 seconds, anterior fontanelle slightly sunken
data_table.Investigations: RSV PCR positive, chest x-ray hyperinflated lungs with no focal consolidation, venous blood gas pH 7.34 pCO2 48 mmHg HCO3 24 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial respiratory support?

options.text: Commence low-flow nasal prong oxygen at 1 L/min and titrate to maintain SpO2 at or above 90 percent
options.rationale: RCH bronchiolitis guidance directs low-flow nasal prong oxygen (typically 1 to 2 L/min) as the first respiratory support with an SpO2 target of 90 percent or above. High-flow is reserved for failure of low-flow after a defined trial. Starting low-flow first prevents premature escalation and the associated nursing acuity. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Commence high-flow nasal cannula at 2 L/kg/min with FiO2 titrated to SpO2 92 to 94 percent
options.rationale: High-flow nasal cannula is reserved for infants who fail low-flow oxygen after 1 to 2 hours of trial or who are showing severe respiratory distress. The SpO2 target in RCH guidance is 90 percent and above, not 92 to 94 percent, so starting at HFNC with a tighter target overshoots the cascade. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Administer nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg with ipratropium bromide 250 micrograms and reassess in 20 minutes
options.rationale: Inhaled bronchodilators are not recommended in bronchiolitis in infants under 12 months because the wheeze is from airway oedema and debris, not bronchospasm. Trials show no clinically meaningful improvement and risk of tachycardia masking deterioration. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Give nebulised 3 percent hypertonic saline 4 mL and reassess respiratory effort in 1 hour
options.rationale: Nebulised hypertonic saline is not recommended in RCH guidance for routine bronchiolitis management; the limited evidence does not support meaningful reduction in admission length, and it can trigger bronchospasm. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Give oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg once daily for 3 days and observe in short-stay unit
options.rationale: Corticosteroids are not indicated in bronchiolitis. Cochrane and RCH guidance both find no benefit on admission rate or length of stay, and there is concern about prolonged viral shedding. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

explanation.summary: Initial oxygen support in moderate bronchiolitis is low-flow nasal prong oxygen titrated to SpO2 90 percent or above, with high-flow reserved for failure of low-flow after a trial period. Bronchodilators, hypertonic saline, and corticosteroids do not change outcomes in typical bronchiolitis under 12 months. The candidate must resist defaulting to the most resource-intensive option early.
key_points: RCH SpO2 target in bronchiolitis is at or above 90 percent, not 92 to 94 percent.
Low-flow oxygen first, HFNC after 1 to 2 hours of failed low-flow.
No role for salbutamol or ipratropium under 12 months in bronchiolitis.
Steroids and hypertonic saline are not recommended.
explanation.pearls: Low-flow nasal prong oxygen at 1 to 2 L/min titrated to SpO2 at or above 90 percent is first-line in bronchiolitis under RCH CPG; reassess at 1 to 2 hours before escalating to HFNC.

sources.label: Cochrane review of bronchodilators in bronchiolitis

stem:
A 2-year-old girl presents to the emergency department at 23:00 with 36 hours of coryza, fever, and a brassy nocturnal cough. Her parents say she has had stridor only when she gets upset and woke her up tonight.

In triage she is settled on her father's lap. There is occasional inspiratory stridor at rest with mild suprasternal retractions, no drooling, and no tripod posture. She tracks faces and is engaged with the toys offered. Air entry is good bilaterally with no wheeze. She has had one previous mild croup episode aged 18 months that settled with no specific treatment.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 132 /min, blood pressure 96/58 mmHg, respiratory rate 32 /min, SpO2 97% on room air, temperature 38.2 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: well perfused, mild suprasternal recession, occasional stridor at rest, good air entry both sides, no drooling, normal voice with brassy cough, normal mental state
data_table.Investigations: clinical diagnosis, no imaging or bloods indicated

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate treatment?

options.text: Oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg as a single dose with discharge planning after a period of observation
options.rationale: RCH CPG categorises this as moderate croup (stridor at rest with mild recessions, no other severity features). The recommended dose is dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg orally for mild or moderate croup; 0.6 mg/kg is reserved for severe croup. Onset is within 1 to 2 hours, and most moderate cases can be discharged after observation. (Source: RCH CPG Croup)

options.text: Oral dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg as a single dose with discharge planning after a period of observation
options.rationale: 0.6 mg/kg is the dose for severe croup with marked recessions, tracheal tug, agitation or cyanosis. Using it for moderate croup over-treats and adds no incremental clinical benefit; RCH recommends the 0.15 mg/kg dose for mild and moderate disease. (Source: RCH CPG Croup)

options.text: Nebulised adrenaline 5 mL of 1 in 1000 with mandatory 4-hour observation in short-stay unit
options.rationale: Nebulised adrenaline is for severe croup or moderate croup not responding to dexamethasone after 30 minutes of observation. Giving it first-line in a child who is stable with mild recessions exposes her to rebound and obliges unnecessary admission. RCH minimum post-adrenaline observation is 3 hours, not 4. (Source: RCH CPG Croup)

options.text: Nebulised budesonide 2 mg as a single dose with discharge planning after a period of observation
options.rationale: Nebulised budesonide has equivalent efficacy to oral dexamethasone in some trials but is more expensive and harder to deliver to an upset toddler. RCH recommends oral dexamethasone as preferred first-line. (Source: RCH CPG Croup)

options.text: Salbutamol 600 micrograms via spacer with 4 puffs every 20 minutes for 1 hour and reassess
options.rationale: Salbutamol treats lower airway bronchospasm, not the subglottic oedema of croup. Stridor that does not change with salbutamol is the textbook finding. Using it here delays effective steroid therapy. (Source: RCH CPG Croup)

explanation.summary: Moderate croup in the RCH classification (stridor at rest, mild to moderate recessions, no agitation or cyanosis) is treated with oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg. The 0.6 mg/kg dose is reserved for severe croup. Nebulised adrenaline is added if severe or if moderate disease does not respond after 30 minutes of observation following steroid.
key_points: RCH dexamethasone dose is 0.15 mg/kg for mild and moderate croup; 0.6 mg/kg only for severe.
Post-nebulised-adrenaline observation is at least 3 hours.
Nebulised adrenaline dose is 5 mL of 1 in 1000 (a flat dose, not weight-based).
Salbutamol has no role in croup.
explanation.pearls: Use dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg orally for mild and moderate croup per RCH CPG; reserve 0.6 mg/kg for severe disease where adrenaline is also given.

subtopic_detail: Severe asthma exacerbation refractory to inhaled therapy
stem:
An 8-year-old girl with a background of moderate persistent asthma on daily fluticasone presents to the emergency department with progressive shortness of breath despite three salbutamol pMDI doses at home over the last hour. Her mother says she has needed her reliever six times today.

After triage she has been given continuous nebulised salbutamol 0.5 percent for 1 hour, nebulised ipratropium 250 micrograms three doses, and oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg. After 60 minutes she remains tachypnoeic, can only speak in short phrases, and SpO2 has improved from 86 to 91 percent on 6 L/min oxygen by mask. She is alert and cooperative. There are no features of impending respiratory arrest.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 148 /min, blood pressure 108/64 mmHg, respiratory rate 36 /min, SpO2 91% on 6 L/min via face mask, temperature 37.0 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: tripoding, marked subcostal and intercostal recessions, prolonged expiratory phase with bilateral wheeze, no silent chest, able to speak in 3-word phrases
data_table.Investigations: venous blood gas pH 7.32 pCO2 46 mmHg HCO3 23 mmol/L (24-32), peak flow not assessable, chest x-ray hyperinflation with no pneumothorax

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Intravenous aminophylline 5 mg/kg loading dose over 20 minutes with cardiac monitoring
options.rationale: Aminophylline is reserved for asthma not responding to magnesium and salbutamol infusion. It has a narrow therapeutic window and significant cardiac and CNS toxicity, so it sits later in the cascade. (Source: RCH CPG Asthma acute)

options.text: Intravenous salbutamol 5 micrograms/kg bolus over 5 minutes followed by infusion at 1 microgram/kg/min
options.rationale: IV salbutamol is an option after IV magnesium has been trialled or when magnesium is unavailable or contraindicated. It carries higher risks of hypokalaemia and arrhythmia, so the cascade recommends magnesium first. (Source: RCH CPG Asthma acute)

options.text: Intravenous magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg (maximum 2 g) infusion over 20 minutes with cardiac monitoring
options.rationale: After failure of maximal inhaled therapy and systemic corticosteroid in severe acute asthma, IV magnesium sulfate is the next-line bronchodilator in the AU paediatric pathway. Dose is 50 mg/kg up to 2 g over 20 minutes with cardiac monitoring. Evidence supports reduction in admission and need for ventilation. (Source: RCH CPG Asthma acute)

options.text: Intramuscular adrenaline 10 micrograms/kg of 1 in 1000 into the anterolateral thigh
options.rationale: IM adrenaline is reserved for severe asthma with impending respiratory arrest or where IV access is not yet established. The child has IV access already and is not in arrest. (Source: RCH CPG Asthma acute)

options.text: Intubate and commence pressure-controlled ventilation with permissive hypercapnia
options.rationale: Intubation is reserved for impending respiratory arrest (silent chest, exhaustion, falling GCS, marked acidosis). The child is alert, cooperative, and improving on inhaled therapy. Premature intubation in severe asthma worsens outcomes. (Source: RCH CPG Asthma acute)

explanation.summary: Severe acute asthma not responding to continuous bronchodilator, ipratropium, and systemic steroid escalates to IV magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg up to 2 g over 20 minutes. IV salbutamol and aminophylline are subsequent rungs of the ladder. Intubation is reserved for impending arrest features which are absent here.
key_points: IV magnesium dose in paediatric severe asthma: 50 mg/kg (max 2 g) over 20 minutes.
Order: continuous salbutamol + ipratropium + oral steroid -> IV magnesium -> IV salbutamol -> IV aminophylline.
Silent chest, exhaustion, falling GCS = impending arrest, intubate.
IM adrenaline only if no IV access or impending arrest.
explanation.pearls: When inhaled therapy plus oral prednisolone fails in paediatric severe asthma, the next step is IV magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg up to 2 g over 20 minutes with cardiac monitoring; IV salbutamol and aminophylline follow.

subtopic_detail: RCH UTI imaging cascade after first typical UTI
stem:
An 18-month-old previously well girl is reviewed in the paediatric outpatient clinic two weeks after her first ever urinary tract infection. The infection was treated with 7 days of trimethoprim. She had fever to 38.6 degrees Celsius for 48 hours, responded clinically by hour 36 of antibiotics, and was systemically well at discharge from the short-stay unit.

MSU at presentation grew Escherichia coli sensitive to trimethoprim. Her antenatal renal ultrasound at 20 weeks was normal. She has had a healthy growth trajectory and is fully toilet trained. Examination today is normal.

data_table.Examination: growing along the 50th centile for weight and height, abdomen soft and non-tender, no flank tenderness, no palpable bladder, external genitalia normal
data_table.Investigations: MSU at presentation grew E coli with sensitivities to trimethoprim and cephalexin, no current symptoms or repeat MSU performed, prior antenatal anomaly scan reported normal kidneys bilaterally

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Arrange a micturating cystourethrogram (MCUG) within 4 to 6 weeks to screen for vesicoureteric reflux of any grade
options.rationale: MCUG is invasive, involves bladder catheterisation and significant radiation, and is not recommended after a single typical UTI in RCH guidance. It is also outside the RCH cascade as DMSA is. (Source: RCH CPG UTI)

options.text: Arrange a renal tract ultrasound within 6 weeks to screen for structural anomalies and post-infective changes
options.rationale: Routine renal ultrasound after a single typical UTI is not in the RCH cascade for children over 6 months who had a clinical response and a normal antenatal scan. Imaging here would only be indicated if features of atypical UTI (poor response to 48 hours of antibiotics, non-E coli organism, sepsis, abdominal mass, or raised creatinine) were present. (Source: RCH CPG UTI)

options.text: Arrange a dimercaptosuccinic acid (DMSA) scan in 4 to 6 months to look for cortical defects and renal scarring
options.rationale: DMSA and MCUG are not in the RCH UTI imaging cascade for paediatric AU practice. They are derived from NICE pathways; following RCH consistently is preferred over mixing AU and UK protocols. (Source: RCH CPG UTI)

options.text: Reassure the family that no further investigation is required and provide written advice on prompt re-presentation if recurrent symptoms develop
options.rationale: RCH guidance does not recommend renal ultrasound after a single typical UTI in a child over 6 months who responded promptly to antibiotics and had antenatal monitoring. Imaging is reserved for atypical UTI, recurrent UTI, or infants under 6 months without antenatal monitoring. (Source: RCH CPG UTI)

options.text: Commence prophylactic trimethoprim 2 mg/kg nocte for 6 months until paediatric nephrology review at that time
options.rationale: Antibiotic prophylaxis is not recommended after a first typical UTI in a child over 6 months. The PRIVENT trial and RCH guidance found no meaningful benefit, with antimicrobial resistance risk; reserve for selected children with recurrent UTI and reflux. (Source: RCH CPG UTI)

explanation.summary: In a child older than 6 months with a single typical E coli UTI that responded to oral antibiotics and a documented normal antenatal renal ultrasound, RCH guidance does not require renal tract imaging or prophylactic antibiotics. Imaging is reserved for atypical features, recurrent UTI, or infants under 6 months without antenatal monitoring. Atypical features include poor response to 48 hours of antibiotics, non-E coli organisms, sepsis, abdominal mass, or raised creatinine. DMSA and MCUG sit outside the RCH cascade and are not pursued in this scenario.
key_points: RCH does not recommend routine renal ultrasound after a single typical UTI over 6 months of age.
Atypical UTI = poor response to 48 h antibiotics, non-E coli, sepsis, abdominal mass, or raised creatinine.
DMSA and MCUG are not in the RCH cascade.
Antibiotic prophylaxis after a single UTI is not indicated.
explanation.pearls: After a first typical paediatric UTI in a child over 6 months with antenatal monitoring documented, no renal tract imaging and no antibiotic prophylaxis are required under RCH CPG.

subtopic_detail: Suspected airway foreign body diagnostic pathway
stem:
A 2-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department by his grandmother. While he was eating cashew nuts at lunch he had a sudden episode of coughing and choking. He recovered within a few minutes but has had a persistent dry cough and intermittent wheeze ever since, for about 4 hours.

He is currently calm, breathing comfortably, and feeding sips of water without distress. Inspection shows no stridor or drooling. Auscultation reveals decreased air entry on the right with localised wheeze on that side. The grandmother says he was choking on a whole nut, not a piece, and that no one performed back blows or any manoeuvres.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 122 /min, blood pressure 98/60 mmHg, respiratory rate 28 /min, SpO2 96% on room air, temperature 36.9 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: alert and playful, no stridor or drooling, reduced air entry on right with monophonic wheeze on right, no recessions, no fever
data_table.Investigations: inspiratory and expiratory chest x-ray shows air trapping on the right with mediastinal shift to the left on expiration, no opacity visible

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Trial inhaled salbutamol via spacer for 1 hour and review with a repeat chest x-ray before deciding on referral
options.rationale: Treating localised wheeze with bronchodilators when foreign body is the leading diagnosis delays definitive care and risks the object moving into a more compromising location. Salbutamol does not displace an inhaled foreign body. (Source: RCH CPG Foreign body airway)

options.text: Arrange computed tomography of the chest with low-dose protocol prior to making a definitive surgical referral
options.rationale: CT can be confirmatory in equivocal cases but adds delay and radiation when the history and inspiratory or expiratory films are already diagnostic. The next intervention is therapeutic (rigid bronchoscopy), not further imaging. (Source: RCH CPG Foreign body airway)

options.text: Urgent referral to ENT or paediatric surgery for rigid bronchoscopy under general anaesthesia in theatre
options.rationale: The combination of witnessed choking, persistent unilateral signs, and x-ray demonstrating ball-valve obstruction is diagnostic of an airway foreign body until proven otherwise. Definitive diagnosis and removal both occur at rigid bronchoscopy under general anaesthesia. Cashews are radiolucent so plain films cannot exclude the diagnosis. (Source: RCH CPG Foreign body airway)

options.text: Perform five back blows followed by five chest thrusts to attempt to dislodge the suspected foreign body
options.rationale: Back blows and chest thrusts are first-aid for an actively choking child with a partially or completely obstructed airway. The child is now stable with localised signs, so blind manoeuvres can move the object centrally and create complete obstruction. (Source: ANZCOR Paediatric BLS guideline)

options.text: Admit for ward observation overnight with a repeat chest x-ray in 12 hours before deciding on intervention
options.rationale: Observation without removal delays definitive diagnosis and increases the risk of distal migration, complete obstruction, or pneumonitis from oils in the nut. Rigid bronchoscopy is the standard of care once foreign body is the leading diagnosis. (Source: RCH CPG Foreign body airway)

explanation.summary: Witnessed choking with persistent unilateral respiratory signs and air trapping on expiratory chest x-ray is a clinical diagnosis of inhaled foreign body. Cashews are radiolucent, so plain films cannot rule it out. Rigid bronchoscopy under general anaesthesia is both diagnostic and therapeutic.
key_points: Inspiratory and expiratory films show air trapping on the affected side in ball-valve obstruction.
Cashews and peanuts are radiolucent; absence of opacity does not exclude airway foreign body.
Back blows and chest thrusts are first-aid for active choking, not stable children with localised signs.
Rigid bronchoscopy under general anaesthesia is the standard of care.
explanation.pearls: Persistent unilateral wheeze and chest x-ray air trapping after witnessed choking go straight to rigid bronchoscopy under general anaesthesia; do not delay for CT or trial bronchodilators.

stem:
A 9-month-old boy presents with 12 hours of episodic distress described by his parents as starting suddenly, drawing his knees up, and screaming, then settling for 15 minutes before the next episode. The last episode included a single dark red jelly-like stool. Between episodes he is pale and quiet.

In the emergency department abdominal examination during a quiet phase reveals a long thin lump in the right upper part of his tummy. Bedside ultrasound shows a target sign in the right upper quadrant. Paediatric surgery and interventional radiology are on site. Two attempts at pneumatic reduction are now complete; the second attempt confirmed only partial reduction of the intussuscipiens with persisting ileocolic invagination.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 156 /min, blood pressure 92/58 mmHg, respiratory rate 32 /min, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 37.1 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: settled between episodes, mildly pale, soft abdomen with palpable elongated mass in right upper quadrant, no peritonism, well-perfused peripheries
data_table.Investigations: ultrasound target sign in right upper quadrant, full blood count haemoglobin 112 g/L (110-140) white cells 11.4 x10^9/L (4-12), venous lactate 1.6 mmol/L (under 2)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Administer broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics and re-image at 12 hours before deciding on theatre
options.rationale: Antibiotics are appropriate as adjunct cover once a decision for surgery is made, but they are not a substitute for operative reduction. Waiting 12 hours for re-imaging risks bowel ischaemia and perforation. (Source: RCH CPG Intussusception)

options.text: Attempt a third pneumatic reduction under deeper sedation after a 1-hour rest
options.rationale: RCH guidance limits enema attempts to two or three depending on local protocol, but continued attempts in the face of partial reduction increase perforation risk and delay definitive care. With failure documented and partial reduction only, theatre is now the standard. (Source: RCH CPG Intussusception)

options.text: Transition to hydrostatic saline reduction under ultrasound guidance as a salvage attempt
options.rationale: Switching modality (hydrostatic versus pneumatic) is not generally salvage after a documented partial reduction failure; centres pick one approach per local resources. Persisting with imaging-guided reduction now adds delay. (Source: RCH CPG Intussusception)

options.text: Admit for observation with serial abdominal examination and repeat ultrasound in 6 hours
options.rationale: Conservative observation after a failed reduction is not appropriate because the bowel remains at risk of ischaemia and perforation. The intervention has not resolved the invagination. (Source: RCH CPG Intussusception)

options.text: Proceed to theatre for laparotomy with manual reduction and bowel viability assessment
options.rationale: After two failed attempts at pneumatic or hydrostatic reduction in a haemodynamically stable child without perforation, the next step is operative reduction. The surgeon will perform manual reduction and resect non-viable bowel if necessary. Continuing to attempt reduction beyond two failed cycles increases the risk of perforation. (Source: RCH CPG Intussusception)

explanation.summary: Failed image-guided reduction of intussusception (typically after two attempts or in the face of partial reduction) is an indication for operative reduction. The surgical team performs manual reduction in theatre and resects non-viable bowel. Delaying further or trying additional non-operative attempts raises perforation risk.
key_points: Two failed enema attempts (or persistent partial reduction) is a surgical indication.
Perforation risk rises with repeated enema attempts.
Operative approach is laparotomy or laparoscopy with manual reduction.
Cover with antibiotics is adjunct, not a temporising substitute.
explanation.pearls: After two failed pneumatic reduction attempts or persistent partial reduction in intussusception, take the child to theatre for laparotomy and manual reduction; further enema attempts increase perforation risk.
